title: The Cabin

jokes.text: David walks in on his parents having sex and screams 'Where's my robe?!'
jokes.setup: David barges into his parents' room and witnesses something traumatic
jokes.punchline: His immediate instinct is to demand his robe rather than flee, a perfectly David-Rose priority
jokes.explanation: The robe line is the perfect David Rose reaction — it's self-centered, absurd, and completely in character. The comedy is in the specificity of his priorities: not fleeing, not covering his eyes, but demanding his comfort item. Solid character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David! Knock!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're indisposed!

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I refuse to feel guilty about being in bed with my wife.'
jokes.setup: His children are both screaming in disgust at catching their parents having sex
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dignified, principled refusal to be ashamed — delivered completely straight — is funnier than any outrage
jokes.explanation: Johnny's dignified, reasonable stance amid absolute chaos is perfectly in character. His refusal to be shamed while everyone around him melts down is a clean comedic beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What did I see? I saw the end of civilization. I saw people I know engaging in behavior that can only be described as... primal. Someone was eating directly from a serving bowl with their hands. With their *hands*. There was no plate involved. No utensil. Just... fingers and dip. It was like watching society collapse in real time.

jokes.text: David: 'Shame on you for attempting that position at 8 o'clock in the morning.'
jokes.setup: David has been screaming in disgust; the joke pivots from the act to the scheduling
jokes.punchline: The implication that the position itself is fine but the time is the real offense — a completely David Rose moral hierarchy
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the opening sequence. The misdirection is excellent — David shifts his objection from what they were doing to WHEN they were doing it, implying he would have no complaint at a more appropriate hour. It's pure David Rose logic: not prudish, just scheduling-conscious. Highly quotable, perfectly structured, and reveals character beautifully. Comparable to an 8+ anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm an adult. I have needs. I have a right to my privacy and my dignity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, no one's denying you that.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, then why do I feel like I'm sneaking around like some sort of teenager?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you are sneaking around. You closed the blinds and locked the door at 2 PM on a Tuesday.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Exactly! Because I'm embarrassed! And I wouldn't be embarrassed if you people weren't making me feel ashamed of being a sexual being.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're not making you feel ashamed. You're making us feel uncomfortable by doing it while we're eating lunch downstairs.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, if you weren't judging me, I wouldn't feel the need to hide it, and if I didn't feel the need to hide it, you wouldn't know about it, and then you wouldn't be uncomfortable!

jokes.text: Moira: 'Do you know how difficult it is for us to share a wall with you two?' — David responds: 'I do now.'
jokes.setup: Moira complains about the indignity of sharing a wall with the couple
jokes.punchline: David's flat, devastated 'I do now' — acknowledging shared suffering while still being horrified
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly placed. The economy is exceptional — it validates Moira's complaint while simultaneously being the funniest possible acknowledgment of mutual trauma. Clean and sharp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The specific judgment about timing and position

jokes.text: Roland: 'You and the wife wanna bump the old uglies, huh?'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Roland simply to fix the door lock for privacy
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately and bluntly reduces the request to its most crude implication
jokes.explanation: Very much a Roland joke — blunt, unseemly, and completely missing normal social cues. Functional character comedy but relies on Roland's established crudeness rather than anything inventive.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I need you to fix the door. I'd like some privacy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Privacy? You want me to put a lock on that thing so nobody can get in? Well, that's gonna cost you extra, buddy. And let me tell you, I've seen some things in this town that would make your hair curl. Just last week, I caught Herb trying to—well, never mind. Point is, privacy ain't cheap. But I got just the thing for ya. I'll put in a lock so tight, even you won't be able to get out. Ha!

jokes.text: Roland: 'You've been out to sea for a while, you wanna bring the boat into port and maybe... get your mast scrubbed.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to be sympathetic about Johnny's supposed intimacy problems
jokes.punchline: The extended nautical metaphor for sex, delivered with genuine helpfulness, is both absurd and oddly specific
jokes.explanation: The nautical metaphor is more creative than simple crudeness — it's Roland expressing genuine concern through his own bizarre lexicon. 'Get your mast scrubbed' is an unexpected and funny specific detail. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, it's like sailing. You gotta know when to drop your anchor, when to hoist your sails, when to let the wind... fill your... canvas.

jokes.text: Roland tells Jocelyn 'Sex life's in the crapper' the moment Jocelyn walks in
jokes.setup: Johnny is embarrassed by the entire conversation; his neighbor's wife suddenly appears
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately summarizes Johnny's private situation to Jocelyn with zero hesitation, betraying any implicit confidence
jokes.explanation: Roland broadcasting Johnny's 'private' problem to Jocelyn the instant she arrives is a perfect encapsulation of Roland's social blindness. The speed and bluntness of 'sex life's in the crapper' is excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think we've covered everything there is to know about—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So what's your favorite position?

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'And feel free to use the restraints.' / 'We keep 'em under the bed.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is warmly encouraging Johnny to take the cabin; the conversation has been about rekindling romance
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn casually mentions restraints under the bed, which both horrifies Johnny and reveals unexpected TMI about the Schitts' sex life
jokes.explanation: The two-beat delivery (Jocelyn mentions restraints, then Roland clarifies their location) is well-structured. Jocelyn's warm, helpful tone makes it more disturbing. Good escalation from a kind offer to a horror.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: How is Johnny doing?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, he's doing okay. He's been having some stomach issues. I think it's from the stress. He's been very constipated. I gave him some of my fiber supplement, but nothing's really moved yet, if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Pick a safe word.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to escape the conversation; Roland has already mentioned restraints
jokes.punchline: Roland's parting advice — completely deadpan — is 'pick a safe word,' delivered as practical wisdom
jokes.explanation: Three words doing enormous work. The deadpan delivery as practical marital advice, following naturally from the restraints reveal, is the perfect button to the entire sequence. Highly quotable and a callback that earns its place.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, and we have some extra items in the bedroom closet if you need them. There are a few massage wands, a rabbit, and some other things that are really just for relaxation.

jokes.text: John and Moira share a romantic moment; David walks in AGAIN looking for a magazine, triggering: 'Oh my God! Am I being punished for something? David, are you dirty peeping tom?'
jokes.setup: The episode opened with David accidentally walking in on his parents; now he does it again
jokes.punchline: The repetition is itself the joke — Moira pivots instantly from romantic to accusatory, calling him a 'dirty peeping tom'
jokes.explanation: Good use of the callback structure — David walking in on intimacy again so quickly is funny, and Moira's 'dirty peeping tom' accusation is a sharp character reversal (she's the one who was embarrassed earlier, now she's offended by him).

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You know, the key is to make sure you have a safe word. Something you'll remember, but something he won't expect. I use 'pineapple.'

jokes.text: 'If you tell me that you're pregnant, I'm gonna vomit on this floor right now.'
jokes.setup: John and Moira have called the kids for 'very important news'
jokes.punchline: David's preemptive threat to vomit, framed as a matter-of-fact warning rather than a joke
jokes.explanation: The specificity — 'on this floor, right now' — elevates this above a generic horror response. It's stated flatly, as if it's beyond his control, which is pure David Rose. The cold open context makes it especially earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We need to take the cabin. Do you know what happens when couples don't have their own space? They start to resent each other. First, it's small things—he leaves his socks on the floor, she rearranges his sock drawer out of spite. Then it escalates: separate bathrooms, separate bedrooms, separate houses, separate continents, and before you know it, you're dividing up the children like they're assets in a hostile takeover, and honestly, at that point, you might as well just go ahead and split the atoms.

jokes.text: David: 'Why don't you guys stay at the cabin for longer?' / Moira: 'I would never just abandon you two in a motel in the middle of nowhere.'
jokes.setup: Parents announce a one-night absence; David immediately pushes for longer
jokes.punchline: Moira's prim refusal to 'abandon' her children — from a woman who has been emotionally absent for most of their lives — played completely straight
jokes.explanation: The irony of Moira worrying about abandonment given her parenting history is a soft but effective joke. Works better for viewers who know the characters well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I didn't realize you were—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David! Must you always be watching us? It's voyeurism!

jokes.text: David: 'Didn't you once take the wrong baby home from preschool?' / Moira: 'Alexis looked Chinese as an infant. How many times must I defend myself?'
jokes.setup: Moira claims she would never abandon her children; David challenges her parenting track record
jokes.punchline: Moira confirms the story is TRUE and defends herself with an absurdly specific and deeply questionable justification
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke. The structure is perfect: David makes an outrageous accusation, and the punchline is that Moira CONFIRMS it and has clearly rehearsed her defense ('How many times must I defend myself?'). The specificity of 'Alexis looked Chinese as an infant' is both absurd and perfectly Moira — completely oblivious to how this sounds. High originality, perfect character integration. Earns its 8.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We have some very important news.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, we do.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're getting divorced.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? No.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're both dying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God. Because if you were both dying, I would kill myself.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Is that the tickle trunk?' — referring to the Roses' luggage
jokes.setup: The Roses have a lot of luggage for a one-night trip; Roland inspects it
jokes.punchline: Roland, having already revealed his restraints and safe words, immediately assumes the fancy luggage contains sex accessories
jokes.explanation: Roland's worldview projecting onto the Roses is consistent and funny. The innocent-sounding 'tickle trunk' as euphemism is a good detail, and the callback to earlier sexual context earns it.

jokes.text: Johnny (muttered aside): 'Yet another disturbing look into his world.'
jokes.setup: Roland has just warned about sex-gadget electrical capacity
jokes.punchline: Johnny's quiet, weary, utterly resigned editorial on Roland's worldview
jokes.explanation: Johnny's straight-man asides are a reliable comedic device. 'Yet another disturbing look into his world' is a good line — acknowledges the pattern, implies there's a whole history, resigned rather than outraged. Works well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was wearing my reading glasses. The babies all look the same when you're not wearing your reading glasses.

jokes.text: David insisting his New York apartment was very clean: 'I kept it clean after Cecilia cleaned.' — then immediately announcing he needs space from Alexis
jokes.setup: David complains Alexis's mess makes him anxious; claims his NY apartment was famously clean
jokes.punchline: The admission that 'clean' meant 'after the maid cleaned' — a perfect oblivious-rich-person reveal
jokes.explanation: The distinction between 'I kept it clean' and 'after Cecilia cleaned' is a small, well-observed bit of rich-person obliviousness that characterizes David precisely. Shows rather than tells.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I just need to get to the store. Can you give me simple directions?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh sure, bud. So you're gonna go down Main Street, but not all the way down Main Street, you're gonna hang a left at the old Kabin, you know, before it got all fancy, then you're gonna see a tree, or maybe it was there last week, could've been cut down, but if it's there, you're gonna go right, and if it's not there, you're still gonna go right, because that's where the store is, but sometimes it moves around depending on inventory, so you might wanna call ahead.

jokes.text: Alexis (talking head): 'Okay. I'll just chill here in my own space and... Yeah. Maybe I'll do a mask or something and... check in with myself and see how I'm doing...'
jokes.setup: David has announced he needs space from Alexis; she's trying to be okay with it
jokes.punchline: Alexis's visible struggle to seem fine about being alone, trailing off into a face mask plan, reveals how utterly lost she is without company
jokes.explanation: The performance is carrying this one — the trailing-off is a character beat that implies Alexis has never spent meaningful time alone. The face mask as an attempt at self-care is a good specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I kept my apartment immaculate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You had a housekeeper come twice a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and I kept it clean between visits.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I miss being surrounded by loose acquaintances who think that I'm... funny, and smart, and charming.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains she misses her old life and wants to do something social
jokes.punchline: The specific qualifier 'loose acquaintances' — not friends, not loved ones — reveals that Alexis's social world was built on surface-level validation
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully written character joke. 'Loose acquaintances' is the operative comedy — Alexis doesn't say she misses friends, she misses people who think she's interesting without actually knowing her. The slight pause before listing the qualities she wants reflected back is also excellent — she knows she's being shallow. High character integration, quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if I don't clean what's already clean, how will I know it's still clean?

jokes.text: David's 'games night' vs. party negotiation — David: 'Something small. Like a little games night.' Alexis: 'Yes, like a small, little games night party.' David: 'No. Like a little games night period.'
jokes.setup: David agrees to something but immediately tries to limit its scope; Alexis tries to expand it
jokes.punchline: The semantic battle over whether 'games night' equals 'party' — David's refusal to acknowledge the party element while it clearly is going to be a party
jokes.explanation: The sibling negotiation dynamic is well-observed. David's insistence on 'games night period' is character-specific — he wants control. The comedy is in the inevitable outcome being obvious while they argue semantics.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm looking forward to space. It's the only place where I won't have to share a bed with Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: We share a bed?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Every night. You steal all the covers and you hog the pillow.

jokes.text: David: 'Any more [than six] and game play gets too yelly.' / Alexis: 'It gets very yelly.'
jokes.setup: David explains his limit of six people for games night
jokes.punchline: His specific complaint about 'yelly' game play — and Alexis earnestly agreeing — reveals they've both had this experience while implying a shared history of chaotic social events
jokes.explanation: The word 'yelly' is doing the heavy lifting here — it's a perfectly David neologism, and Alexis adopting it immediately shows their sibling wavelength. Small but efficient.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to treat myself to a facial mask tonight. I need to decompress.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's wonderful, darling. Which one are you using?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The one I ordered from that spa in Switzerland. It's supposed to be incredibly restorative.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How long do you leave it on?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Forty-five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And how will you answer the door if a client calls?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...I'll reschedule them.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: With what excuse? 'I'm sorry, I can't meet with you, I'm wearing a mud mask'?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Actually, that's exactly what I would say.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, there's your relaxation ruined.

jokes.text: John and Moira arrive at a beautiful cabin — 'Not bad! Rustic but nice.' — then cut to: the key is not under the mat
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira marvel at the surprisingly nice cabin Roland offered
jokes.punchline: The key is not under the mat — Roland gave them completely wrong directions or address; they're at the wrong cabin
jokes.explanation: The visual beat of them praising the cabin followed by the missing key is a good structural turn. The setup (Roland's complicated directions) primes us for a navigation problem, so this payoff works.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, if you're not going to let me throw a party, then I'm going to make this room so disgusting that you'll have no choice but to let me move out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How is that going to work?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to start eating crackers in bed.

jokes.text: Johnny's increasingly furious muttering: 'He's a moron! Key's under the mat, is it? The key is under the mat! Agh! That's great! Lying son-of-a... Agh! Idiot man! Moron!'
jokes.setup: Johnny discovers the key isn't under the mat at a cabin that isn't even Roland's
jokes.punchline: His escalating, sputtering rage — barely able to form insults, circling back to 'moron' — is a perfect Johnny breakdown
jokes.explanation: Eugene Levy's physical and verbal sputtering is a series trademark. The escalating near-profanity ('lying son-of-a...') with self-censoring is good character comedy. The repetition of 'moron' as his ultimate insult is appropriately restrained-but-furious.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just miss having people around me who actually cared about what I had to say, you know? People who valued my opinions and my thoughts.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But honestly? I also kind of miss the shallow relationships where nobody actually knew me well enough to judge me for my mistakes.

jokes.text: Moira initiates a romantic role-play: 'Well hellooooo. Oh, my car broke down and it's raining, and I wondered if I might spend the night.' / Johnny: 'Well, first let's get you out of those... dry clothes and we'll see where the night takes us.'
jokes.setup: After breaking into a stranger's cabin, Moira launches into a romantic role-play fantasy
jokes.punchline: Johnny joins enthusiastically but immediately fumbles the cliché ('dry clothes' instead of 'wet clothes') — he's trying but not great at this
jokes.explanation: Johnny saying 'dry clothes' is the gem — it's a Freudian/nervous slip that reveals he's earnestly trying but bad at this. The contrast between Moira's theatrical commitment and Johnny's fumble is very them. Also the absurdity of role-playing in a stranger's cabin they broke into.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No more than eight people. It's a Friendsgiving, not a Friendsgivings.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And everyone has to be gone by 9 PM. I can't have people 'round-abouting' in my living room at all hours.

jokes.text: David's games night casting conversation with Stevie — 'Oh, all those types of people move away from here.' / David: 'That's funny.' / Stevie: 'No, I'm serious.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to find smart, funny, knowledgeable people for games night in a small town
jokes.punchline: Stevie flatly says all such people have left — and means it sincerely — which is both a town commentary and funnier for being true
jokes.explanation: This is a perfectly Stevie joke — deadpan, honest, self-aware about the town without being self-pitying. Her 'No, I'm serious' refusal to let it be a joke is funnier than the observation itself. Strong character integration for both characters.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I've got it. Left at the intersection, then straight for about two miles.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm perfectly fine. I don't need to stop and ask for directions.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I know exactly where I'm going.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, this should be it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is... this is not it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland said left at the intersection. This is clearly not the intersection.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going back. I'm going back to find the intersection.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Where is the intersection? There's no intersection here!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I am never taking directions from Roland Schitt again. Never.

jokes.text: David: 'He finished high school.' (as Eric's only qualification) — Stevie: 'I don't love his look.' David: 'Oh, looks are important? Oh... Why don't we throw a pageant?'
jokes.setup: David is listing requirements for games night; Stevie suggests Eric
jokes.punchline: David's only stated qualification for Eric is completing high school; Stevie dismisses him for his look; David mocks her for caring about looks while he implicitly cares about intelligence
jokes.explanation: The 'finished high school' as an endorsement is a small but effective small-town joke. The pageant quip is sharp. The exchange shows David and Stevie's quick wit dynamic forming.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland said the key would be under the mat. It's not under the mat. It's not under the planter. It's not under the rock shaped like a sleeping dog, which, by the way, is just a regular rock. Where is this key?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have been standing in the rain for fifteen minutes looking for a key that apparently doesn't exist, which is very on-brand for this town and very on-brand for Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So now I'm wet, I'm frustrated, and I have a social disease from touching that rock.

jokes.text: David: 'She get it?' / Stevie: 'Get what?' / David: 'Like "get it" get it. It, like the vibe.' / Stevie: 'Can I be on your team? You sound really fun.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to explain an ineffable quality he wants in games night participants
jokes.punchline: Stevie's completely dry 'Can I be on your team? You sound really fun.' — dripping with sarcasm about how exhausting David is being
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan sarcasm is perfectly calibrated here. She doesn't explain the joke — she just delivers the mock enthusiasm and lets it hang. This is Stevie at her best, and it also defines the David/Stevie dynamic that becomes central to the series.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, here we are. Our cabin.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's... quaint.

dialogue.speaker: TV Voice
dialogue.line: Oh yes, that's it. Right there. Oh my God, yes!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I believe the previous tenants left their... entertainment on.

dialogue.speaker: TV Voice
dialogue.line: You like that, don't you? You dirty girl!

jokes.text: David asks if Sarah 'gets it' — 'Like get it get it. It, like the vibe.' / Stevie: 'Can I be on your team? You sound really fun.'
jokes.setup: David is asking whether a potential games night recruit understands the particular energy he wants
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'Can I be on your team? You sound really fun' — heavy sarcasm delivered flatly, mocking his elaborate games-night anxiety
jokes.explanation: Good Stevie deadpan. 'You sound really fun' as sarcasm aimed at someone being incredibly uptight about a fun activity is a clean reversal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I want to invite smart, funny people. People who are engaging and culturally aware.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, that eliminates basically everyone in this town.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Roland has gay friends! Again, shocked and delighted.' — misreading the photo of Roland with Bob and Gwen
jokes.setup: Moira sees a photo of Roland with Bob and his wife and assumes it's a same-sex couple
jokes.punchline: Moira's progressive enthusiasm ('shocked and delighted!') paired with being completely wrong about what she's seeing
jokes.explanation: 'Shocked and delighted' is a perfect Moira phrase — performative allyship delivered theatrically. The joke gently satirizes a certain kind of performative progressivism while remaining warm. Her enthusiasm being wrong is the comedic engine.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Eric seems qualified.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Eric? No. Absolutely not.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'They just don't know any better.' — re: why the people in the photo look happy
jokes.setup: Moira asks what Bob and Gwen have to be happy about; Johnny says they have no money issues
jokes.punchline: Johnny's instinctive condescension about simple happiness — 'they just don't know any better' — reveals his own still-present wealth snobbery even as he lives in a motel
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical line that simultaneously makes Johnny look ridiculous (he's broke and living in a motel) while being completely in character. The irony is earned rather than underlined.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Looks matter for games night.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, well then we should probably just make it official and start ranking people by their appearance at the door.

jokes.text: David: 'And how are your... Trivia skills?' — after learning Eric was Stevie's babysittee
jokes.setup: David finds out Eric was a child that Stevie babysat — meaning he's young and inexperienced
jokes.punchline: David's slight pause before pivoting to test Eric's trivia skills, his face presumably falling, is a perfect reaction beat
jokes.explanation: The pause and pivot is doing the work here — we understand David is recalibrating his expectations. The 'My what?' from Eric completing the exchange confirms the fear.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You should enter a pageant.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A pageant? I... that's actually not the worst idea you've ever had.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I brought this pie from work but on the way, I realized I didn't check what kind so there's a 50/50 chance there's meat in it.'
jokes.setup: Twyla arrives as a guest bearing a gift
jokes.punchline: The pie is either sweet or has meat — she genuinely didn't check — and she presents this as a normal disclosure
jokes.explanation: A perfect Twyla introduction — cheerful, oblivious, and completely specific. The 50/50 meat pie is an absurdist detail that couldn't come from anyone else and immediately establishes her as a character. The casualness of the delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, she's not... she's very talented, but she's not really the caliber of guest that we're looking for.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What caliber is that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you know, someone who's... accomplished. Refined. Someone with a certain... je ne sais quoi.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Sarah has all of those things.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but not in the way that I need.

jokes.text: David's increasing horror as guests arrive late and he recites the schedule: 'It's 8:15. It's 8:15. We should have started at 8. So... fifteen minutes late, let's sit down...'
jokes.setup: Games night is supposed to start at 8; it's now 8:15
jokes.punchline: David's mounting anxiety about fifteen minutes — stated twice — reveals his control-freak nature in miniature
jokes.explanation: The repetition of '8:15' is the joke — David saying it twice as if the second repetition will fix it. Pure anxious control freak character comedy. Specific to David but not especially inventive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Games need to run a certain way. There are rules.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh wow, David Rose needs things to be a certain way? That's so surprising. I had no idea you were controlling.

jokes.text: Eric: 'I don't think I can fit ten names on this piece of paper.' — when asked to write ten names
jokes.setup: David explains the game: write ten names on pieces of paper, one per paper
jokes.punchline: Eric misunderstands completely — he thinks they're all going on one paper — confirming his intellectual limitations
jokes.explanation: A gentle, character-consistent joke from Eric. The misunderstanding is entirely plausible and reveals his limitations without being cruel. Not inventive but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This place looks like a domestic terrorist's hideout... if the terrorist had given up and decided to just live here.

jokes.text: Eric: 'Who should I put down?' / David: 'I can't tell you that, Eric, 'cause that would wreck the game.' / Eric: 'So then how do I know what to put?'
jokes.setup: Eric can't figure out how to fill out the slips on his own
jokes.punchline: The circular logic of Eric genuinely not understanding how to think of famous people independently — it's not stupidity theater, it's real confusion
jokes.explanation: The escalation from one Eric confusion to a second confirms his character while building a running bit. David's barely contained horror is the emotional undertone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Roland! Is that you with another man? I had no idea you were so... progressive.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? No, that's my brother!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's even more progressive.

jokes.text: Eric: 'Can you put yourself down?' — asking if he can write his own name as a famous person
jokes.setup: Eric can't think of any famous names to write
jokes.punchline: His solution is to write himself — completely sincere, completely oblivious
jokes.explanation: This is the best Eric joke in the sequence. The sincerity of his self-nomination as a 'famous name' is both absurdist and perfectly character-specific. The question itself is the punchline — one word could have followed it ('yes' or 'no') but instead everyone just reacts.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not a photo of us together.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it should be. You're always together.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how photos work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, in gay relationships, you're supposed to be joined at the hip. I assumed you'd also be joined at the camera.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I may or may not have invited a few other people.' / David: 'What kind-who?' / Alexis: 'I don't know. I met them on the way home.'
jokes.setup: A knock at the door during David's controlled games night
jokes.punchline: Alexis not only invited strangers but doesn't know who they are — she met them in transit
jokes.explanation: The escalating revelation structure works well: additional guests → she doesn't know them → she met them on the way home. Each beat escalates David's nightmare. Very Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, they look happy because they don't know what's about to happen to them. Ignorance is bliss.

jokes.text: David (re: Dane): 'Who the... Bleep... is this?'
jokes.setup: Dane arrives as an uninvited guest
jokes.punchline: David's self-censored profanity ('Bleep') — he substitutes the word 'Bleep' rather than saying an actual profanity, which is both prissy and funnier
jokes.explanation: Saying 'Bleep' as an actual word rather than an actual profanity is a great David Rose detail — he's outraged enough to swear but too composed to actually do it. The word 'Bleep' itself said aloud is funnier than any actual profanity would have been. Strong character integration and a quotable delivery.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How do you know Stevie?

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: Oh, we met in high school.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: High school? Stevie went to high school here?

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was a senior when she was a freshman.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You were a senior when she was a freshman? So you're like... significantly older than her.

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: I mean, not that significantly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Eric, that's deeply inappropriate.

jokes.text: Moira (talking head after role-play): 'I can't see out of one eye. But I'm still alive, that's the main thing.'
jokes.setup: Moira has returned from what was clearly an eventful night at the cabin
jokes.punchline: She's been partially blinded somehow but frames 'still alive' as the benchmark — deeply theatrical and self-dramatic
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent Moira line. The combination of genuine injury ('can't see out of one eye') and theatrical understatement ('that's the main thing') is perfect for her. We don't know what happened, which makes it funnier. 'Still alive' as the consolation prize for a romantic evening is rich comedy. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I brought pie from the cafe. It's, uh, it's blueberry. Or at least that's what they told me.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Though, honestly, I'm not entirely sure what went into it. One of the new guys was making the filling, and I didn't want to ask too many questions.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I will get some food, I will get the wine. You... have done more than enough.' — to Moira after the eye incident
jokes.setup: Moira is sitting injured; Johnny takes over
jokes.punchline: The weighted pause before 'You... have done more than enough' — implying Moira's contribution to the night was somehow catastrophic
jokes.explanation: The pause and emphasis are everything here. Johnny's resigned chivalry — taking care of the situation while subtly blaming Moira — is a good character beat. The mystery of what happened amplifies it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, so the rules are simple. You have to think of a number between one and a hundred, and I have to guess it.

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: Oh! I thought you meant I had to think of a number and then you would guess what I was thinking before I thought of it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not... that's not how that works.

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: Well, that would be impressive.

jokes.text: Bob and Gwen are found sitting in the cabin reading, having watched Johnny and Moira's entire romantic role-play
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira have been conducting an elaborate romantic role-play in what they think is their cabin
jokes.punchline: The cabin belongs to Bob and Gwen, who have been quietly present the whole time, reading
jokes.explanation: This is the best structural joke in the episode. The reveal is perfectly set up — we knew the cabin was wrong, but we forgot about it during the role-play, so the audience shares the Roses' horror. Bob and Gwen reading quietly while a couple performed a seduction scenario for them is both cringe-perfect and well-earned. The structure (planted, forgotten, revealed) is textbook misdirection. Earns its 8.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you still don't understand the rules?

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to figure out where everyone puts their balls.

jokes.text: Bob: 'Quite a while. Yeah, you seemed to have a little trouble there, getting going.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks how long Bob and Gwen have been in the cabin
jokes.punchline: Bob cheerfully mentions they witnessed the hesitant start to the role-play — the detail confirms they heard everything
jokes.explanation: Bob's specific recall of 'a little trouble getting going' — referring to the awkward role-play startup — deepens the cringe perfectly. His matter-of-fact reporting of this is funnier than outrage would be. A great extension of joke #54.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, they're here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Who is 'they'? And why do they seem intent on ruining my evening?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Just... be nice. They're not that bad.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, I have formed a very strong opinion about these people, and I have formed it quickly.

jokes.text: Bob: 'No, they're a mile up the road. Big tree, a fence. You can't miss it.' — pointing out they're in the wrong cabin entirely
jokes.setup: Moira insists this is Roland and Jocelyn's cabin
jokes.punchline: Bob's helpful directions, delivered with no malice, casually reveal the Roses were at the completely wrong location — the embarrassment is total
jokes.explanation: The callback to Roland's confusing directions is satisfying. Bob's helpfulness (the landmark is a big tree and fence) mirrors Roland's overconfident simplicity. A good structural payoff.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Games night is a structured event with specific rules and etiquette.

dialogue.speaker: Dane
dialogue.line: Oh, I brought some party supplies to make it fun.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Party supplies? This isn't a party, it's games night.

dialogue.speaker: Dane
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. I brought streamers and a disco ball.

jokes.text: David: 'You can't say mother! You can't say mother!' — after the winning clue 'She's a mother and has your cousin's name' leads to 'Mother Teresa'
jokes.setup: Someone gives an illegal clue containing the word 'mother' and it wins them the point
jokes.punchline: David's outrage at the rules violation — invalidating a correct answer that came from cheating — while everyone else is celebrating
jokes.explanation: David enforcing game rules while losing his mind is very him. The specific rule violation (you can't say part of the name) shows the writers actually know the game, which adds authenticity to the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my god, what happened to you?

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: I fell down the stairs at the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are you okay? Do you need to go to the hospital?

dialogue.speaker: Eric
dialogue.line: No, I'm fine! It's just a little bump. Nothing to worry about!

jokes.text: Dane: 'This party's terrible.' — as a guest who wasn't invited and brought a keg
jokes.setup: The games night has devolved into chaos; an uninvited guest with a keg expresses dissatisfaction
jokes.punchline: The audacity of an uninvited party-crasher criticizing the party he crashed
jokes.explanation: The pure nerve of it is the joke. Dane's obliviousness mirrors Roland's on a younger scale. The short, blunt delivery maximizes the impact.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland assured me we were going the right way. He said 'You can't miss it.'

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, we've been driving for three hours. I'm pretty sure we missed it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: He was very confident about his directions.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Johnny, we're in a completely different county. We're not even in the right province anymore.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Every time the vein in my brother's eye twitches, we take a drink.'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests a drinking game; Alexis immediately has a specific one prepared based on David's stress response
jokes.punchline: She has clearly been monitoring her brother's stress vein all evening and has already gamified it
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent joke. The concept — that David's stress is so visible and consistent that you can build a drinking game around his eye vein — is both character-specific and original. The fact that Alexis proposed it instantly, without hesitation, means she's been thinking about this all night. High originality and character integration. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you can't just act out the entire movie! There are rules.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David, it's just charades.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Just charades? JUST charades? There are very specific guidelines that exist for a reason. You're supposed to use gestures and pantomime, not dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I was doing an accent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: An accent is not a gesture, Alexis.

jokes.text: 'Every time the vein in my brother's eye twitches, we take a drink.'
jokes.setup: Dane suggests a drinking game; David is visibly stressed
jokes.punchline: Alexis proposes using David's stress-eye-twitch as a drinking game trigger — weaponizing his anxiety for entertainment
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. It's simultaneously a drinking game proposal, a sibling roast, and a reveal that David has a stress eye-twitch serious enough to be a reliable game mechanic. The specificity ('vein in my brother's eye') and the casual cruelty of Alexis's delivery make it exceptional. Highly quotable. Strong character integration — only Alexis delivers this with this combination of warmth and brutality.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We could play a drinking game where we take a shot every time David does that thing with his face.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What thing with my face?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That stressed out thing. Like this—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You'd be drunk in like five seconds.

jokes.text: David: 'Why would you bring up my eye in front of all these people?'
jokes.setup: Alexis just announced the eye-twitch drinking game to a room full of people
jokes.punchline: David's horrified objection reveals that the eye twitch is apparently a known, embarrassing condition — his indignation is about disclosure, not denial
jokes.explanation: David's wounded dignity is a good reaction beat. He doesn't deny the vein, just objects to its public exposure — which confirms it's real and chronic.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, boring is literally like death. It's the same thing. You're just sitting there, existing, and nothing is happening, and you're dying inside. So really, I'm protecting everyone by throwing a party.

jokes.text: 'But I have a queen bed next door with my name on it and I have been looking forward to sleeping in that bed all day, so...'
jokes.setup: Alexis is appealing to David to stay at the chaotic party
jokes.punchline: David exits by citing his primary motivation: the queen bed — not moral high ground, not concern for anyone, just the bed
jokes.explanation: David's exit motivation being 'the queen bed I've been thinking about all day' is perfectly David — self-interest trumping any social obligation, delivered with the gravity of a principled stand.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we could always play a drinking game based on David's stress levels.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not a game, that's a cry for help.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Every time David gets anxious, we take a shot.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'd all be dead by dinner.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'So his eye twitched five times, so drink up, bitches!'
jokes.setup: David has left; the party continues with the eye-twitch drinking game still in effect
jokes.punchline: Stevie has been keeping count and is now running the game — her adoption of Alexis's chaos is a character turn
jokes.explanation: Stevie counting five eye twitches and announcing it with 'drink up, bitches' is a good callback that shows her warming to the chaos. The callback earns its place and 'bitches' is a funny choice of address for this specific group of people in a motel.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: The door is locked because I locked it.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I was trying to have a private moment.

jokes.text: The Roses being locked inside a cabin that isn't theirs — Bob locks the door to prevent strangers coming in, not realizing the strangers are already inside
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira try to leave Bob and Gwen's cabin
jokes.punchline: They're locked IN — Bob locked the door to stop strangers entering, without knowing strangers were inside
jokes.explanation: Bob's explanation — 'yes, it's locked to prevent strangers from coming into the cabin. We didn't realize strangers would already be in the cabin when we locked it' — is a beautifully logical piece of absurdism. The logic is perfectly valid and the situation perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, you have to come back. The motel is falling apart without you. I'm serious. I found a family of raccoons living in the ice machine, and I named them after your family members because I was so lonely.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Also, I've been competing with the Holiday Inn down the road, and if I don't beat them by next quarter, I'm going to have to eat a tire.

jokes.text: Moira's gracious exit: 'You've been consummate hosts, really. Fantastic day.'
jokes.setup: The Roses are being let out of a cabin they broke into, where they performed an involuntary sex role-play for strangers
jokes.punchline: Moira deploys her society-hostess mode to compliment Bob and Gwen on being 'consummate hosts' — as if she were the guest and they threw a lovely party
jokes.explanation: This is Moira Rose at her absolute peak. The instinct to perform grace and gratitude in a situation of total humiliation — calling strangers 'consummate hosts' when she was the intruder — is perfect character writing. 'Fantastic day' with total sincerity after being partially blinded, locked in, and exposed is the kind of line that defines a character. Exceptional character integration. Among the best jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I need your help with something.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Of course. What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm competing in this thing and I need to win. I can't lose.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, well you've come to the right person because I also cannot lose at anything. It's a compulsion.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So we're perfect for each other then.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Believe it or not, we are in the middle of a very intense game of drunken charades and my team is losing. Badly.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks David to come back inside; he's reluctant
jokes.punchline: The confession that she, the most self-possessed person in the room, is losing badly at a children's party game — and the 'believe it or not' framing — is a great self-aware moment
jokes.explanation: Stevie admitting vulnerability — badly losing at drunken charades — while maintaining complete deadpan delivery is good character work. The 'believe it or not' framing acknowledges how unlikely this is for her.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, well, well... looks like someone's got a leaky pipe.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I will happily let you get back to this big queen bed if you'll just help us win a few more rounds. Just need somebody sober.'
jokes.setup: David is attracted to the queen bed his parents left behind; Stevie uses it as leverage
jokes.punchline: The bar is low — she needs someone 'sober' — which implies how bad the current situation is
jokes.explanation: The 'just need somebody sober' as the qualification reveals the party has deteriorated beyond recovery. Functional joke, carries character dynamics forward.

jokes.text: Moira initiates a second role-play — this time Johnny is 'Eddie' the handyman: 'Well, thank you very much, Mrs. Rose. I don't often get compliments when I'm fixing doors.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is fixing the lock; Moira sees an opportunity for a second role-play fantasy
jokes.punchline: The role-play now casts Johnny as a working-class tradesman — 'Eddie' — and Moira slips naturally into the fantasy, this time in their actual home
jokes.explanation: The role-play motif returning for the episode's ending is a strong callback that also deepens character. Moira's 'blue collar' fetish (she compliments him on it) and Johnny's surprisingly easy entry into the 'Eddie' persona is both funny and sweet. The episode comes full circle from the failed cabin trip to a successful role-play at home.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, Edward, I'm afraid I must tell you, I shan't be able to pay you today.' / Johnny/Eddie: 'Well, that's okay, Mrs. Rose. Maybe there's um... some other way you can pay me, if you get my drift.'
jokes.setup: The Eddie role-play escalates as Moira introduces a 'payment' problem
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'if you get my drift' — played completely straight as Eddie — is both cringe and charming; he's actually getting into it
jokes.explanation: The role-play working — both of them in it — is the joke. The cliché 'pay in other ways' plot is funny because it's so earnest. Johnny committing to 'if you get my drift' with a straight face is the best part.

tmdb_overview: Looking for privacy, Johnny and Moira take the Mayor up on his offer of using his cabin for a night, while Alexis and David use the opportunity to throw a party.
insight: Cringe escalation drives a middle-tier episode; 53 jokes land at modest 2.09-per-minute pace.
